calendar.matters:
Hearing On Writ Of Mandate As To Respondent California Dmv
Off Calendar Jun-04-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Petitioner Richard Lau's petition for writ of mandate from the suspension of his driving privilege is denied. Mr. Lau fails to show that respondent DMV abused its discretion or its findings are contrary to the weight of the evidence. The DMV properly admitted the DS-367 officer's statement under Evidence Code 1280 as a public employee record. (Jackson v. Department of Motor Vehicles (1994) 22 Cal.App.4th 730, 737.) The failure of Officer McFall to sign the first page of the officer's statement that referred to objective symptoms of intoxication does not render the statement untrustworthy and inadmissible under Evidence Code 1280. The presumption that Officer McFall regularly performed his duty per Evidence Code 664 is unrebutted and other portions of the officer's statement clearly show that Officer McFall observed objective indicia of Mr. Lau's intoxication. (AR 13.) The weight of the evidence supports the DMV's finding that Mr. Lau was driving. The DMV properly admitted Ranger Mitra's observations of driving. Mr. Lau admitted that he had been drinking and was trying to go home when he spoke with Officer McFall. (AR 13; see also AR 20.) These statements are admissible as party admissions. Ranger Mitra's statements about Mr. Lau are admissible under Government Code 11513(d) to supplement or explain Mr. Lau's admissions. (Lake v. Reed (1997) 16 Cal.4th 448, 461-462.) The DMV established that Mr. Lau's blood-alcohol content was at .08% or higher based on the officer's statement, the recorded test results, and the presumption of Evidence Code 664. (Taxara v. Gutierrez (2003) 114 Cal.App.4th 945, 949.) Mr. Lau did not produce any affirmative evidence that casts doubt on these facts. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600 =302/hk
